reasoning: The exploitation chain requires two capability gates, not one. Gate 1: init() at line 58 checks current_user_can('edit_theme_options') before dispatching to _import(). Gate 2: the 'cei-importing' nonce required by wp_verify_nonce() at line 251 is generated exclusively inside the includes/control.php template, wrapped in an if(CEI_Core::can_import()) block (line 15) which requires current_user_can('install_plugins'). This nonce is never generated outside that block — not in any AJAX handler, REST endpoint, or other code path. An attacker with only edit_theme_options cannot obtain a valid 'cei-importing' nonce because the nonce field (wp_nonce_field on line 24 of control.php) is never rendered to them. Without the nonce, _import() returns early at line 251. Therefore the effective gate is install_plugins capability, which in a default WordPress install is restricted to administrators only. This is an admin-to-admin privilege boundary violation: an authenticated administrator can write arbitrary options beyond customizer scope, but cannot escalate further from a lower-privilege role. The original finding's reachability claim (install_plugins required) turns out to be correct once the nonce gate in the UI template is traced — the adversarial verdict's correction (edit_theme_options is the gate) is only partially right, applying only to the capability check in init(); the nonce gate enforces install_plugins independently. Since install_plugins is admin-only in default WordPress, an unauthenticated or low-privilege attacker cannot breach this gate. Severity is downgraded from HIGH to LOW.
